questions.question: In the motor hierarchy, which neurons specify WHAT movement to make AND HOW to make it?

options.A: Lower motor neurons in the spinal cord
options.B: Upper motor neurons in the motor cortex

questions.explanation: Upper motor neurons in the motor cortex specify both WHAT movement to execute and HOW to execute it, forming the top of the motor hierarchy that descends through basal ganglia and cerebellum to lower motor neurons.

questions.question: What did the classic cat experiment with spinal transection demonstrate about Central Pattern Generators?

options.A: Cats cannot walk without cortical input
options.B: Spinal circuits can produce rhythmic walking movements without higher brain input
options.C: The cerebellum is essential for all locomotion

questions.explanation: After spinal transection (cutting off higher brain input), the cat's hindlimbs could still produce coordinated walking movements on a treadmill, demonstrating that Central Pattern Generators — spinal circuits — can generate rhythmic motor patterns independently.

questions.question: The readiness potential in motor cortex appears:

options.A: At the same moment as the conscious urge to move
options.B: Several seconds AFTER the conscious urge to move
options.C: Several seconds BEFORE the conscious urge to move

questions.explanation: The readiness potential appears seconds BEFORE a person reports the conscious urge to move. This finding has profound implications for free will — the brain begins preparing a movement before we are consciously aware of wanting to move.

questions.question: What is anosognosia in the context of motor control?

options.A: Inability to plan complex movement sequences
options.B: Patients with premotor/motor cortex damage who are unaware of their inability to move
options.C: Loss of sensation in the contralateral limb
options.D: Tremor at rest caused by basal ganglia dysfunction

questions.explanation: Anosognosia occurs when patients with damage to premotor or primary motor cortex are unaware of their inability to move. They genuinely do not recognise their motor deficit.

questions.question: Cortical motor axons cross to the opposite side of the body at the:
questions.explanation: Cortical motor pathways decussate (cross over) at the medullary pyramids in the brainstem. This is why the left motor cortex controls the right side of the body and vice versa — contralateral control.

questions.question: In Georgopoulos' (1986) population coding experiment, how is movement direction determined?

options.A: By the single neuron with the highest firing rate
options.B: By a majority vote across a population of neurons
options.C: By the neuron closest to the target location
options.D: By inhibition of opposing movement neurons

questions.explanation: Georgopoulos (1986) demonstrated population coding: each neuron has a preferred direction but fires for a range of directions. The actual movement direction is determined by a 'majority vote' — the vector sum of activity across the entire neural population.

questions.question: Which basal ganglia pathway DISINHIBITS movement, allowing it to proceed?
questions.explanation: The direct pathway through the basal ganglia disinhibits movement — it releases the thalamus from tonic inhibition by the globus pallidus, allowing desired movements to proceed. The indirect pathway provides additional inhibition to suppress unwanted movements.

questions.question: Parkinson's disease is caused by the death of neurons in the:
questions.explanation: Parkinson's disease results from the death of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra. This leads to reduced dopamine in the striatum, causing tremor at rest, rigidity, and bradykinesia. L-dopa treatment partially compensates for the dopamine loss.

questions.question: Unlike cortical motor pathways, the cerebellum controls movement on the:

options.D: It does not directly control any side

questions.explanation: The cerebellum controls movement ipsilaterally — on the same side of the body. This contrasts with cortical motor pathways, which cross at the medullary pyramids for contralateral control. Damage to the right cerebellum therefore affects right-sided movements.

questions.question: Hemiballismus (violent involuntary flinging movements) results from damage to the:
questions.explanation: Hemiballismus is caused by damage to the subthalamic nucleus, which is part of the basal ganglia's indirect pathway. When the subthalamic nucleus is damaged, its excitatory drive on the globus pallidus is lost, leading to excessive disinhibition and violent involuntary movements.
